title: Ice Thickness Basics
subtitle: How to judge safe ice and what to avoid

sections.heading: Minimum Thickness
sections.body: Clear, solid ice needs at least 4 inches (10 cm) for walking alone. Snow ice is weaker — treat it as half the strength of clear ice. Always verify locally; thickness varies across a lake.

sections.heading: Warning Signs
sections.body: Cracks, open water near inlets, dark or honeycombed ice, and recent warm spells are red flags. Avoid areas with current, bridges, and pressure ridges.

sections.heading: Travel in Pairs
sections.body: Spread weight, carry throw ropes, ice picks, and a whistle. Tell someone your plan and expected return time.

sections.heading: If You Break Through
sections.body: Face the direction you came from — that ice held your weight. Kick horizontally, use ice picks, roll away from the edge to spread load.

sections.heading: After Immersion
sections.body: Remove wet layers, insulate, seek shelter, warm core first. Medical evaluation is wise after significant cold exposure.

title: Augers & Cutting Ice
subtitle: Hand vs power augers and sharp blades

sections.heading: Choosing an Auger
sections.body: Hand augers are light and quiet; power augers save energy for many holes. Keep blades sharp and cover points during travel.

sections.heading: Hole Size
sections.body: 6–8 inch holes suit most panfish and walleye. Larger holes help big fish but remove more ice structure — plan accordingly.

sections.heading: Shelters
sections.body: Flip-overs and hub shelters cut wind and extend comfortable fishing time. Secure shelters in wind and avoid heater exhaust buildup.

sections.heading: Layering
sections.body: Moisture-wicking base, insulating mid, wind shell. Carry spare gloves and dry socks in a sealed bag.

sections.heading: Contour Basics
sections.body: Fish often relate to depth changes, weed edges, and hard/soft bottom transitions. Use maps and sonar to find consistent travel lanes.

sections.heading: Early vs Late Ice
sections.body: Early ice may concentrate fish shallow; mid-winter often pushes fish deeper or to classic feeding shelves.

sections.heading: Start Subtle
sections.body: Begin with small lifts and long pauses. Increase aggression only if fish follow but won’t commit.

sections.heading: Deadstick Option
sections.body: A still bait on a nearby rod often catches fish that ignore an active jig.

title: Tip-Ups and Flags
subtitle: Spreading out and managing multiple lines

sections.heading: Spacing
sections.body: Separate tip-ups to reduce tangles and cover more water. Check regulations for line and hook limits.

sections.heading: Hooksets
sections.body: Give predator fish a moment to turn with bait, then a firm sweep — avoid immediate jerks on delicate bites.

sections.heading: Keeping Bait Alive
sections.body: Insulated buckets, aerators, and frequent water changes help in cold weather. Match hook size to bait size.

sections.heading: Artificial Options
sections.body: Soft plastics and tungsten jigs shine when live bait is restricted or finicky fish need a slower show.

sections.heading: Prime Times
sections.body: Dawn, dusk, and cloudy days often outproduce mid-day glare periods. Focus on breaks and transitions rather than featureless flats.

sections.heading: Presentation
sections.body: Glow jigs, rattle spoons, and live minnows on a deadstick are classic one-two combinations.

title: Perch & Panfish Schools
subtitle: Finding roaming pods and staying on them

sections.heading: Mobility
sections.body: Schools move — if bites stop, drill new holes in steps along structure until you reconnect.

sections.heading: Light Line
sections.body: Thin line and small jigs improve bites in clear water under ice.

sections.heading: Selective Harvest
sections.body: Keep what you will use; release oversized breeders when regulations encourage it. Handle fish with wet hands and minimize air exposure.

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.body: Licenses, seasons, and special rules change by waterbody. Verify before each trip.

sections.heading: Safety First
sections.body: Picks, rope, compass or GPS, first aid, headlamp, spare batteries.

sections.heading: Fishing Kit
sections.body: Extra jigs, line, pliers, depth tool, sonar battery, and a notebook to log what worked.
